title: Calculating how much loft insulation you need
subtitle: A worked example for a typical UK home
excerpt: Most UK lofts have 100-150 mm of existing insulation. Current best practice is 270-300 mm. This is the calculation that tells you how many packs to buy and what U-value you will achieve.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Part L1B (2022) for the 0.16 W/m2K loft target. Energy Saving Trust loft-insulation guidance for typical-loft-depth statistics and pack coverage figures.

glossaryTerms.definition: A measure of heat flow through a building element, in watts per square metre per degree Kelvin (W/m2K). Lower is better: a U-value of 0.16 loses half as much heat as one of 0.32.

The thermal conductivity of an insulation material, in W/mK. Lower lambda means the material conducts less heat; a thinner layer achieves the same U-value. Mineral wool is typically 0.040 W/mK; PIR board is around 0.022 W/mK.
Part L of the Building Regulations (England and Wales): Fuel and Power Conservation. Sets minimum thermal performance standards for new builds and significant retrofits.
Glass or rock fibre insulation supplied as batts, rolls, or loose fill. The standard loft-insulation material in the UK, with a lambda value of around 0.040 W/mK.

content.text: Most UK lofts have between 100 mm and 150 mm of
mineral wool
on the floor. Part L1B of the Building Regulations targets a finished
U-value
of 0.16 W/m2K for loft insulation upgrades. To hit that target with mineral wool (lambda 0.040 W/mK), you need 250-270 mm of total depth. Most lofts need a top-up of 120-170 mm.
What you need to measure first

Existing depth: measure the insulation currently in the loft at three or four points (centre of bay, near the eaves, near the hatch). Use a ruler pushed down to the joist top. Note the lowest reading.
Loft floor area: measure length and width of the loft space from inside. Subtract the area covered by cold-water tanks or obstructions.
Joist depth: measure the depth of the floor joists. Standard UK domestic joists are 100 mm or 125 mm deep. This tells you how much of the top-up sits above the joists.
The calculation
The target total depth depends on the
lambda value
of the insulation you are buying. The formula is:
Target depth (mm) = (lambda / target U-value) x 1000

For mineral wool at lambda 0.040 and a target U-value of 0.16: (0.040 / 0.16) x 1000 = 250 mm. For sheep wool at lambda 0.038: (0.038 / 0.16) x 1000 = 238 mm. For a loose-fill blown product at lambda 0.044: (0.044 / 0.16) x 1000 = 275 mm.
If you already have 100 mm in place, you need 150-170 mm of top-up to reach 250-270 mm total. Existing insulation is fully credited: the top-up sits directly on top of it.
Worked example
A 1930s semi in Leeds. Loft floor area 42 m2. Existing insulation: 100 mm of compressed mineral wool between 100 mm joists. Buying standard mineral wool roll at lambda 0.040.
Target total depth: (0.040 / 0.16) x 1000 = 250 mm.
Top-up needed: 250 mm minus 100 mm existing = 150 mm.
A 150 mm roll covering 5 m2 per roll: 42 m2 / 5 = 8.4 rolls. Buy 9 rolls to account for waste and the area around the hatch.

First layer: 100 mm batts between the joists (flush with joist top). Second layer: 150 mm rolls laid across the joists at 90 degrees to the first layer. This is the standard two-layer method that avoids cold bridges through the joists.
Common variations
Loft with boarding

Boarding laid directly on top of insulation compresses the wool and destroys its performance. The insulation under boarding in a typical UK loft is compressed to around 50 mm and performs at roughly U 0.50, four times worse than the target. The solution is to raise the boarding on legs or bearers to clear 270 mm of insulation beneath it. Proprietary loft-leg systems exist for this; alternatively, cut 100 x 50 mm timber bearers and bolt them through the joists.
Chalet bungalow or room-in-roof

Where part of the loft is habitable, the insulation target moves to the roof slope rather than the floor. This changes the calculation: a warm-roof method uses PIR board or a high-performance mineral wool between and below the rafters, and the target U-value of 0.16 still applies to the finished assembly. A separate tutorial covers insulating below rafters for a warm-roof conversion.
North-facing or cold loft

The target depth does not change based on orientation. What changes is the urgency: a north-facing loft on an exposed hillside site loses more heat per degree than the same loft in a sheltered southern suburb. The calculation is the same; the payback is faster.
When to call a professional

Loft insulation top-up is the most DIY-friendly retrofit available. Call a professional if the loft has structural issues, if wiring needs moving first, or if the roof shows moisture damage. ECO4 funding and council schemes sometimes cover the cost; check eligibility before buying materials.
Done well versus done badly

Done well: 270 mm across the full floor to the eaves. The cold-water tank is wrapped on sides and lid (not underneath). The hatch board is insulated to the same depth as the floor, and the hatch frame has a draught-proof brush seal. Wiring is buried in the insulation.

Done badly: the insulation is patchy, the eaves are unfilled (often blocked by old insulation roll rather than filled to the eaves ventilation baffle), the cold-water tank sits on uninsulated boards with the lid missing, and the hatch board is a plain piece of plywood with no seal. These are the failure modes the
Part L
inspector checks at new-build. In an existing house, they are your checklist.
